title_en: Marriage and Family Law in Al-Sharh al-Kabir
content_en:
Ad-Dardiri's chapters on marriage and family law in Al-Sharh al-Kabir constitute one of the most detailed and practically important sections of the work, reflecting the Maliki school's sophisticated approach to personal status law — an area in which Maliki fiqh has been historically dominant in North and West Africa and retains significant influence in contemporary Muslim family law.

Marriage (nikah) in the Maliki school is a contract that requires several conditions for validity. The Maliki school requires the presence of a wali (guardian) for the validity of the marriage of a virgin, whether she is young or adult. However — and this is a significant Maliki distinction — an adult non-virgin woman (thayyib) may contract her own marriage without a guardian's presence, though the guardian's participation is still preferred. This is more permissive than the Shafi'i position (which requires a wali for all women regardless of prior marital status) but more restrictive than the Hanafi position (which permits an adult woman to contract her own marriage without a guardian in all cases).

The mahr (dower) is an obligatory right of the wife that becomes her exclusive property. The Maliki school does not specify a minimum amount but does specify a minimum that places the gift in a dignified rather than derisory category. The mahr may be deferred entirely or in part, but the wife is entitled to withhold herself from cohabitation until the prompt portion of the mahr is paid.

The Maliki school has a distinctive treatment of the waiting period ('iddah). A divorced woman who has been in a valid marriage and has been alone with her husband must observe a waiting period of three menstrual cycles (if she menstruates) or three months (if she does not). A widow observes four months and ten days. A pregnant woman observes her 'iddah until delivery, regardless of how long that takes. Ad-Dardiri addresses the evidential basis for each of these rulings in Al-Sharh al-Kabir.

With respect to divorce, the Maliki school recognizes several forms. Talaq is the husband's unilateral right to divorce. Khul' is a divorce initiated by the wife in exchange for compensation (usually the return of the mahr). The Maliki school also recognizes judicial divorce (faskh) on the grounds of harm (darar), failure to provide maintenance, absence of the husband, or disease — grounds that have made Maliki personal status law historically more responsive to the needs of women than some other schools in certain respects.

The Maliki school's distinctive treatment of the oath of abstention (ila') and the oath of comparison (dhihar) — both pre-Islamic forms of divorce-like declarations that Islam regulated — is addressed by ad-Dardiri with reference to the specific Quranic verses that governed their transformation in Islamic law.

Custody (hadanah) of children after divorce is addressed in Al-Sharh al-Kabir with Maliki specificity: the mother generally has priority for young children, but custody transfers to the father or his family when boys reach a certain age (around seven or nine years in Maliki positions) and when girls reach puberty or marriage. The mother's right to custody is conditional on her not remarrying a man who is a non-mahram to the child.

Ad-Dardiri's commentary on these sections of Mukhtasar Khalil exemplifies the Maliki school's strength in personal status law: attentive to evidence, aware of the diversity of opinions within the school, and deeply concerned with practical outcomes for Muslim families living under Islamic legal guidance. Al-Sharh al-Kabir remains the primary reference for Maliki family law in the courts of North Africa and West Africa to this day.
content_id:
Bab-bab ad-Dardiri tentang pernikahan dan hukum keluarga dalam Al-Syarh al-Kabir merupakan salah satu bagian yang paling terperinci dan paling penting secara praktis dari karya tersebut, mencerminkan pendekatan mazhab Maliki yang canggih terhadap hukum status personal — sebuah bidang di mana fikih Maliki secara historis dominan di Afrika Utara dan Barat dan mempertahankan pengaruh yang signifikan dalam hukum keluarga Muslim kontemporer.

Pernikahan (nikah) dalam mazhab Maliki adalah akad yang memerlukan beberapa syarat untuk keabsahannya. Mazhab Maliki mensyaratkan kehadiran wali untuk keabsahan pernikahan seorang perawan, baik muda maupun dewasa. Namun — dan ini adalah perbedaan Maliki yang signifikan — seorang wanita thayyib (janda) yang sudah dewasa dapat mengakadkan pernikahannya sendiri tanpa kehadiran wali, meskipun partisipasi wali tetap lebih disukai. Ini lebih permisif daripada posisi Syafi'i (yang mensyaratkan wali untuk semua wanita tanpa memandang status pernikahan sebelumnya) tetapi lebih ketat daripada posisi Hanafi.

Mahar adalah hak wajib istri yang menjadi milik eksklusifnya. Mazhab Maliki tidak menetapkan jumlah minimum tetapi menetapkan minimum yang menempatkan pemberian tersebut dalam kategori terhormat daripada merendahkan. Istri berhak menahan dirinya dari percohabitasian sampai bagian mahar yang segera dibayarkan.

Mazhab Maliki memiliki perlakuan khas terhadap masa iddah. Wanita yang dicerai yang telah berada dalam pernikahan yang sah dan telah berduaan dengan suaminya harus menjalani masa tunggu tiga siklus menstruasi atau tiga bulan. Seorang janda menjalani empat bulan sepuluh hari. Wanita hamil menjalani iddahnya sampai melahirkan.

Berkenaan dengan cerai, mazhab Maliki mengakui beberapa bentuk. Talak adalah hak sepihak suami untuk menceraikan. Khul' adalah cerai yang diprakarsai oleh istri dengan imbalan kompensasi. Mazhab Maliki juga mengakui cerai yudisial (faskh) atas dasar bahaya (dharar), kegagalan memberi nafkah, ketidakhadiran suami, atau penyakit — alasan-alasan yang secara historis membuat hukum status personal Maliki lebih responsif terhadap kebutuhan perempuan daripada beberapa mazhab lain dalam aspek-aspek tertentu.

Komentar ad-Dardiri tentang bagian-bagian ini dari Mukhtashar Khalil mencontohkan kekuatan mazhab Maliki dalam hukum status personal: memperhatikan bukti, menyadari keragaman pendapat dalam mazhab, dan sangat peduli dengan hasil-hasil praktis bagi keluarga-keluarga Muslim yang hidup di bawah panduan hukum Islam. Al-Syarh al-Kabir tetap menjadi referensi utama untuk hukum keluarga Maliki di pengadilan-pengadilan Afrika Utara dan Afrika Barat hingga hari ini.
title_id: Pernikahan dan Hukum Keluarga dalam Al-Syarh al-Kabir
content_ar: Ad-Dardiri's الأبواب on marriage and الأسرة law in Al-Sharh al-Kabir constitute one of the most detailed and practically المهم sections of the work, reflecting the المالكي school's sophisticated approach to personal status law — an area in which المالكي الفقه has been historically dominant in North and West Africa and retains المهم influence in contemporary مسلم الأسرة law. Marriage (nikah) in the المالكي school is a contract that requires several conditions for validity. The المالكي school requires the presence of a wali (guardian) for the validity of the marriage of a virgin, whether she is young or adult. غير أن — and this is a المهم المالكي distinction — an adult non-virgin woman (thayyib) may contract her own marriage without a guardian's presence, though the guardian's participation is still preferred. This is more permissive than the الشافعي position (which requires a wali for all women regardless of prior marital status) but more restrictive than the الحنفي position (which permits an adult woman to contract her own marriage without a guardian in all cases). The mahr (dower) is an obligatory الحق of the الزوجة that becomes her exclusive property. The المالكي school does not specify a minimum amount but does specify a minimum that places the الهبة in a dignified rather than derisory category. The mahr may be deferred entirely or in part, but the الزوجة is entitled to withhold herself from cohabitation حتى the prompt portion of the mahr is paid. The المالكي school has a distinctive treatment of the waiting period ('iddah). A divorced woman who has been in a valid marriage and has been alone with her الزوج must observe a waiting period of three menstrual cycles (إذا she menstruates) or three months (إذا she does not). A widow observes four months and ten days. A pregnant woman observes her 'iddah حتى delivery, regardless of how long that takes. Ad-Dardiri addresses the evidential basis for each of these rulings in Al-Sharh al-Kabir. With الاحترام to divorce, the المالكي school recognizes several forms. Talaq is the الزوج's unilateral الحق to divorce. Khul' is a divorce initiated by the الزوجة in exchange for compensation (usually the return of the mahr). The المالكي school أيضاً recognizes judicial divorce (faskh) on the grounds of harm (darar), الخسارة to provide maintenance, absence of the الزوج, or المرض — grounds that have made المالكي personal status law historically more responsive to the needs of women than some other schools in certain respects. The المالكي school's distinctive treatment of the oath of abstention (ila') and the oath of comparison (dhihar) — both pre-Islamic forms of divorce-like declarations that الإسلام regulated — is addressed by ad-Dardiri with reference to the specific القرآني الآيات that governed their transformation in Islamic law.
